script:
Hello, this is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. Tuesday — here is your QQQ recap. The portfolio closed one point nine nine percent down on the session. It was a broad-based selloff concentrated in semiconductors, with only a handful of names managing to hold positive ground.

Sandisk collapsed more than twelve and a half percent in a single session. That is not a rounding error — that is a memory-sector warning shot, and it landed on a day when chip stocks were already under pressure across the board.

No macro data was available to anchor the session, but the tape spoke clearly. Technology and semiconductors bore the brunt of the selling, consistent with a rotation dynamic that saw investors moving out of high-multiple chip names and into oversold enterprise software. The Nasdaq composite fell sharply on the session, consistent with the portfolio's own one point nine nine percent decline. Defensive and consumer names showed relative strength, but they were not enough to offset the damage in the core holdings.

The deepest single-day story in the portfolio Tuesday was the collapse in Sandisk, which closed at one thousand six hundred seventy-three dollars and ninety-seven cents — down two hundred forty-one dollars and ninety-five cents, a loss of twelve point six three percent. That is a significant single-session move for a memory-focused name. Analysts, however, are not sounding the alarm on the fundamentals. According to reporting from Yahoo Finance, analysts are framing Tuesday's plunge as a buying opportunity, arguing that the memory boom cycle has more runway than the market is currently pricing in. The context matters: Sandisk sits inside a broader semiconductor ecosystem that is dealing simultaneously with export control uncertainty, China demand questions, and rotation pressure out of chip stocks. For holders of Micron — the portfolio's primary memory-sector position — the Sandisk move is a direct read-through. When memory valuations compress this sharply in one session, it rarely stays contained to a single name. That is the key risk to monitor heading into Wednesday.

Now the full rundown of primary holdings.

NVIDIA closed at two hundred three dollars and fifty-three cents, down three point five two percent on the session. That is the largest dollar position in the portfolio, and it was under pressure from multiple directions Tuesday. ASML, the dominant supplier of chip manufacturing equipment, reports quarterly earnings Wednesday morning. Analysts expect the Dutch company to address both capacity constraints and the growing risk from proposed U.S. legislation that would require allied nations to align with American export controls targeting China. ASML has publicly denied selling its most advanced extreme ultraviolet tools to Beijing, but China is still projected to account for up to twenty percent of ASML's revenue this year through lower-specification equipment. The outcome of that earnings call will ripple directly into NVIDIA's own demand narrative. Watch Wednesday's ASML print closely.

Apple closed at three hundred seventeen dollars and thirty-one cents, up zero point six three percent — one of only a few primary holdings to finish in positive territory Tuesday. The constructive price action came alongside a meaningful data point: China smartphone shipments fell four point three percent in the second quarter, the fifth consecutive quarterly decline, according to IDC. Apple was one of only two vendors to grow in that market, posting shipment growth of twenty-four point four percent. That is a material outperformance in a deteriorating environment. Separately, Broadcom's extended chip supply agreement with Apple through twenty thirty-one was in circulation Tuesday, underscoring Apple's long-range silicon procurement strategy. A legal dispute involving OpenAI and trade secrets was also circulating, with Apple named — worth monitoring for any escalation.

Alphabet closed at three hundred fifty-two dollars and fifty-one cents, down one point three one percent. The session loss was relatively contained compared to the broader semiconductor selloff. On the news side, Alphabet announced a collaboration with INVIDI Technologies to integrate Google Ad Manager into unconnected set-top boxes globally, expanding its addressable advertising reach into households that were previously outside digital targeting. That is an incremental revenue story in the company's advertising segment, not a headline mover, but it reflects continued monetization expansion.

Micron closed at nine hundred thirty-seven dollars, down four point three two percent. The Sandisk collapse Tuesday is the directly relevant data point here. Both names operate in the DRAM and NAND memory space, and when one memory name breaks down at that magnitude, it signals either sector-specific pressure or a read-through to inventory and pricing dynamics. Multiple Yahoo Finance reports noted afternoon rotation out of chip stocks into enterprise software names — Micron was caught in that flow. The next meaningful catalyst for Micron is any forward guidance commentary from peers or ASML Wednesday.

Microsoft closed at three hundred ninety dollars and ninety-nine cents, up one point five three percent. That is a clear outperformance relative to the portfolio Tuesday, and it reflects Microsoft's relative insulation from the semiconductor rotation. No specific news catalyst drove the move, but the company's exposure to enterprise software and cloud infrastructure positioned it as a relative beneficiary when investors moved out of chip-heavy names. The stock held its ground cleanly.

Amazon closed at two hundred forty-seven dollars and thirty-one cents, up zero point eight zero percent. Like Microsoft, Amazon held positive on the session, supported by its cloud and consumer logistics profile rather than direct semiconductor exposure. No specific news drove the Tuesday move, but the stock's ability to close higher in a down tape is worth noting. AWS demand commentary remains the primary forward catalyst to track.

Advanced Micro Devices closed at five hundred thirty-four dollars and thirty-nine cents, down four point two one percent. The loss mirrors the broader semiconductor selloff and tracks closely with NVIDIA's percentage decline. Advanced Micro Devices carries meaningful exposure to both data center GPU demand and the China export control risk narrative. No company-specific news drove Tuesday's move — this was sector rotation pressure. The ASML earnings report Wednesday will be a direct read-through for Advanced Micro Devices' own demand picture.

Tesla closed at three hundred ninety-four dollars and seventy-six cents, down three point one nine percent. No specific news catalyst was attributed to the Tuesday decline. Tesla's consumer discretionary classification separates it from the semiconductor rotation story, but the stock sold off in sympathy with the broader risk-off tone. Autonomous vehicle regulatory developments and energy storage commentary remain the primary forward-looking catalysts to track for this position.

Intel closed at one hundred three dollars and twelve cents, down six point one two percent. That is the steepest percentage decline among primary holdings Tuesday. Intel carries the dual burden of its own turnaround execution risk and direct exposure to the export control environment given its manufacturing and China revenue profile. The ASML earnings report Wednesday is particularly relevant here — any commentary on equipment capacity and geopolitical headwinds will land directly on Intel's narrative.

Three data points to track in the next forty-eight hours. First, ASML quarterly earnings, reporting Wednesday morning — the company's commentary on EUV capacity, China sales mix, and U.S. export control legislation will set the tone for the entire semiconductor equipment complex, with direct read-throughs to NVIDIA, Advanced Micro Devices, Micron, and Intel. Second, TSMC is expected to report a fifth consecutive quarter of record profit driven by AI chip demand — that result and any forward guidance will be a primary read on the health of the AI infrastructure buildout underpinning the portfolio's largest positions. Third, monitor any follow-through in memory-sector names after Sandisk's twelve point six three percent single-session decline — analyst commentary calling it a buying opportunity needs to be validated by price stabilization Wednesday.

This is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. We will be back same time the next business day with tomorrow's news. ASML and TSMC both report Wednesday — those two prints may define the direction of the portfolio's largest positions for the rest of the week.
